Chunk Content: # Setting the stage: a journey into the world of entertainment

The entertainment sector has evolved, with consumers now valuing experiences over products. This report examines Indoor Amusement Centers in India, their current state, and future prospects.

Indoor Amusement Centers have transformed over two decades, with international and new local operators introducing premium formats. These centers are now essential to retail developments, driving foot traffic and enhancing appeal.

India has **~6.6 mn sq ft** of operational Indoor Amusement Center space across **500+ centers**. They’re increasingly found in malls, catering to all age groups, and in standalone complexes offering unique experiences.

By 2028, Indoor Amusement Center stock could reach **~11 mn sq ft**. Larger retail developments and malls are expected to accommodate more expansive and innovative centers. Further, standalone Indoor Amusement Center that offer a variety of entertainment concepts under one roof are expected to become increasingly popular in the coming years.

**Rahul Arora**
Senior MD and Head - Retail Services and Office Leasing Advisory, India, JLL

Source: The Game Palacio

Chunk Content: # Beyond shopping: how entertainment is stepping up the retail game

The new world we find ourselves in post-pandemic is marked by significant cultural transformation, evolving lifestyles, and a desire for unique experiences. Consumers now seek retail destinations that serve as social meeting places. This has led to a growing demand for retail developments that provide a comprehensive offering of food, shopping, daily needs, and entertainment all in one location.

In response to changing consumption patterns, developers are focusing on creating differentiated experiences to drive higher footfalls and increased dwell time. The supply data suggests that upcoming retail developments will be larger in size compared to those that became operational in the last decade.

Over the past ten years, the average size of new retail supply was approximately **390,000 sq ft**. However, this is expected to increase by 27% to reach **495,000 sq ft** with the addition of new supply during H2 2024-2028. This signifies a notable trend in the retail market moving towards larger-sized, experience-led developments that have a substantial proportion of entertainment and F&B.

It is seen that Indoor Amusement Centers are becoming one of the major specialty anchors for which shoppers across age groups now plan to visit. Moreover, leisure activities create a halo effect on shopping malls since visitors often come in groups, boosting sales across various retail categories.

Context: This JLL report, published in 2024, examines entertainment as the new frontier in Indian retail across India. This specific section details the historical evolution of Indian Amusement Centers from before the 1980s to 2024, highlighting key developments and trends over different periods.

Chunk Content: # Evolution of Indian Amusement Centers

| Period | Key Developments | Narrative Details |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Before 1980s** | Cinemas were the major source of entertainment | No dedicated organised zones for recreation and leisure. |
| **1980s to 1990s** | Outdoor amusement parks were introduced | Appu Ghar opened in Delhi in 1984, followed by Essel World in MMR in 1989. |
| **1990s to 2000** | Outdoor amusement continue dominance | Indoor gaming was limited, just to arcade gaming in neighborhood markets. Premium bowling alleys marked the initial phase of Indoor Amusement Centers. Outdoor amusement parks continued to grow with development of Nicco Parks in Kolkata, Fun and Food Village, Delhi and Ramoji Filmcity, Hyderabad. |
| **2000-2010** | Quality malls came up, bringing global operators | With the launching of quality malls, indoor amusement centers started to come up. International operators started entering India, including Funcity and Timezone. |
| **2010-2019** | Home-grown operators emerged with newer formats | As malls expanded across India, indoor entertainment centers grew, with mall planners accommodating Family Entertainment Centers (FECs). Urbanization and a growing middle class increased demand for Amusement Centers. Indian operators like Smaaash, Snow World Entertainment and Masti Zone introduced new formats. |
| **2020-2021** | Slowdown in entertainment industry due to the pandemic | Covid period - The entertainment business was the most adversely impacted sector due to covid restrictions. |
| **2022-2024** | Multiple IACs were included in malls with larger allocation to entertainment category and introduction of premium formats | Strong consumer demand has led mall developers to expand entertainment zones, allowing multiple Amusement Centers in one location. Premium centers offering high-end gaming and dining experiences are emerging. |

Chunk Content: # Outdoor Amusement Parks

**Outdoor formats are broadly classified into 2 types:**

### Outdoor Amusement & Theme Parks
*   An entertainment avenue that features various attractions, such as rides and games, as well as other events for entertainment purposes.
*   Feature games, live entertainment shows, themed areas, restaurants, and souvenir shops.
*   Examples include Imagicaa, Wonderla, Worlds of Wonder, Nicco Park and Saiteerth.

Source: Imagicaa

### Outdoor led Amusement Centers
*   Combination of outdoor and indoor entertainment center.
*   Includes outdoor activities like Go karting, golfing etc. along with indoor activities like arcade games, trampoline etc.
*   Many of these formats have an indoor dining area also. Examples include Formula Karting, One Golf, WOOP, Loco Bear and Torq03.

Source: Formula Karting

Context: The JLL report "Game on! Entertainment as the new frontier in Indian retail," published in 2024, analyzes the evolving landscape of Indoor Amusement Centers (IACs) across India. This specific section details the location preferences of IAC operators, emphasizing the importance of compliant real estate spaces and strategic clustering within retail environments.

Chunk Content: # Location and catchment is the key for success of IACs

### Location
Source: Timezone

**Shopping malls preferred for IACs:**
*   Established retail ecosystem
*   Ample parking and F&B options
*   Diverse retail mix attracting footfall

**Mixed-use and standalone complexes gaining prominence:**
*   Preferred by competitive socializing and eatertainment operators
*   Access to a larger customer base-office goers for team bonding and competitive socializing.

**Highway retail corridors embracing entertainment:**
*   New retail hotspots due to infrastructure development
*   Offering diverse retail and F&B options
*   Entertainment operators expanding with indoor/outdoor activities

**Neighborhood retail spaces in residential clusters:**
*   Preferred by Trampoline parks, kids zones and escape rooms
*   Local population as primary catchment.

# Operators are emphasizing on compliant real estate spaces

### Clustering and compliant spaces

**IACs prefer to be in a cluster of F&B restaurants and food court in a mall:**
*   In malls, locations near food courts and restaurants are preferred owing to higher foot traffic.
*   Significant spillover to F&B and other retail categories.

**Operators willing to co-exist:**
*   Operators are open to sharing space with other attractions to create a cluster, provided the themes are varied and appeal to different age groups.

**Fully compliant and high-quality real estate spaces in demand:**
*   Organized entertainment operators prioritize visitor safety, requiring compliant, high-quality spaces typically found in malls. With ~56 mn sq ft of new mall space expected in 5 years, availability of superior venues will increase.
*   Standalone complexes need upgrades to meet compliance standards.

Context: This JLL report, published in 2024, examines the entertainment sector as a new frontier in Indian retail. This specific section details the key challenges faced by operators of Indoor Amusement Centers across India, including capital intensity, real estate availability, and industry non-recognition.

Chunk Content: # Key challenges faced by operators

| No. | Challenge | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Capital intensive business** | Requires high capex in opening Amusement Centers and recurring refurbishment and replacement of games in every 2-3 years to maintain freshness. |
| 2 | **Limited availability of quality real estate space** | Limited availability of quality and fully compliant standalone real estate spaces in desired locations. In tier 2 and 3 cities, availability of quality retail space is still a challenge. |
| 3 | **Limited game manufacturers in India** | Games and rides are mostly imported due to limited manufacturing capability in India. Given the high import duties, the refurbishment and maintenance cost is prohibitive too. |
| 4 | **Seasonal variations in revenue** | Peak periods coincide with events such as summer vacations and regional festive seasons. Low periods of revenue during school examinations and some national holidays. |
| 5 | **Vulnerable to economic uncertainties** | Sensitive to economic uncertainties and disrupting events like the pandemics. Prolonged period of restrictions greatly impact business. |
| 6 | **Industry non-recognition** | Amusement Centers and Parks are not recognized as an industry in India and therefore do not enjoy adequate support and benefits from the government. Securing institutional investment is a big challenge due to non recognition. |
| 7 | **Higher degree of customization needed for growth** | Global operators are mandated to follow their SOPs* in design and offering. Given the challenges of evolving RE landscape in India, customizing the offerings is required for growth. |
| 8 | **Limited innovation** | Domestic operators are limited to innovation and have been replicating global formats majorly due to industry still being nascent and lack of government support which is limiting the industry growth. |

*SOPs: Standard Operating Procedures

These centers are evolving to offer larger spaces, premium experiences, and a range of activities that cater to different age groups.

The expansion of Indoor Amusement Centers allows for a wider variety of entertainment offerings, from traditional arcade games and play zones for children to more immersive experiences such as virtual reality, simulated sports, and interactive attractions.

As the retail market in India continues to evolve, we can anticipate development of more destination-oriented shopping malls which act as social meeting places and entertainment hubs.

As the demand for immersive and diverse entertainment experiences continues to grow, we can expect to see more standalone indoor amusement parks opening up across the country, offering an array of entertainment concepts under one roof.
